entry.title: From Intent to Action: Recap of Session 3 of the Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise Series
entry.content:
The conversation around enterprise AI has a pattern problem. Organizations pour resources into pilots, build enthusiasm around demos, and then watch momentum stall somewhere between proof of concept and production. Session 3 of the Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise webinar series tackled this head-on, not with more excitement about what AI can do, but with a diagnosis of why it so often fails to scale with John Santic and Jason Taylor

We now have more than 6,600 registered attendees across 80 countries, representing over 2,100 unique customer and partner organizations. That reach reflects a genuine appetite for grounded, architecturally serious conversations about AI.

John Santic examined the structural conditions that make enterprise AI fail to scale safely, and the architecture required to fix them. Jason Taylor grounded that framework in two specific SAP capabilities: Joule Work and the SAP Knowledge Graph.

Registration link for access to all the recordings and slide and to join the forthcoming sessions! https://events.sap.com/eaa-autent-web/en_us/home.html

The State of the Market: Ambition Without Architecture

Before diving into solutions, both speakers established why this conversation is urgent. Research cited in the session points to a 2x revenue boost available to organizations that can deliver safe and governed AI at scale. The appetite is real. The execution gap is equally real. The numbers are striking in their imbalance. By the end of 2026, enterprise AI technology spend is expected to reach $209 billion. Governance spend: less than $1 billion. The average AI initiative failure is estimated to cost $4.4 million. Only 21% of organizations have a mature governance model in place. More than half are running AI without any formal oversight.

The First Mile Problem: Why Judgment Cannot Be Improvised

John Santic opened with a provocation that deserves more attention than it typically gets in AI conversations: enterprise work was designed for human execution, not machine execution. That distinction sounds obvious. Its implications are not.

When humans work, they carry an enormous amount of invisible context, what John described as everything below the waterline in an iceberg. Linguistic understanding, semantic relationships between concepts, epistemological judgment about what is actually true versus what was merely told to us, and what Aristotle called phronesis, the practical wisdom to know when a rule applies, when it does not, and what to do in the space between.

AI agents inherit the task. They do not inherit the judgment. They are, as John put it, "prediction machines sophisticated enough to look like judgment but containing none."

Companies fill that gap with prompts, fragments, and markdown files. That patchwork approach produces three specific categories of risk:

  • Single agent context risk. The natural language prompt becomes the primary contract for agent execution. The result is that governable artifacts get compressed into unstructured text with no audit trail, unacceptable for any regulated process.
  • Multi-agent fragmentation risk. As agent networks grow, enterprise knowledge becomes noise stitched together. Agents reason over fragments instead of operational truth. The risk compounds with every additional agent.
  • Prompt sprawl and MD file chaos. What begins as an organized system of instructions quickly degrades into overlapping, duplicated, out-of-date files that bear no reliable relationship to how the business actually operates. It is not a sustainable governance model.

Compounding all three is the economics reality. AI usage is currently heavily subsidized, that will not last. As compute costs normalize and context burdens grow, organizations spending frontier model economics on low-value work will face a hard reckoning. John's phrase for it: "We are baking bread with gold." The conclusion: context cannot be improvised at query time. It has to be engineered as a system before any agent fires.

Context as a System: A Five-Layer Architecture

John Santic's answer is not a governance policy document. It is a governance architecture, a five-layer context stack where each layer resolves a specific dimension of the judgment gap that AI systems cannot fill on their own.

 

  • Layer 1, Data Context.

Every agent draws from the same complete operational record, regardless of where data lives. SAP Business Data Cloud, with Apache Iceberg-native capability, anchors this layer with an open, enterprise-wide data fabric, the most complete operational picture of the business available to any agent at runtime.

  • Layer 2, Semantic and Knowledge Context.

Every agent operates on the same semantic truth about the business. The SAP Knowledge Graph resolves relationships of meaning, master data, behavioral conditions, how concepts connect across domains, so agents do not have to infer or approximate what things mean. This is the layer where agents stop guessing and start reasoning from shared definitions.

  • Layer 3, Process and Predictive Context.

Signavio's process intelligence surfaces not just how human and system workflows run, but how agents are running within them. The Prior Labs acquisition adds a causal dimension, identifying the specific reason a particular outcome will occur before it arrives. This layer also introduces one of the session's most important concepts: process atoms.

Process atoms are behavioral, context-bound units of work. They encode explicit human logic that was never systematized, the tacit rules, exception conditions, and stop criteria that experienced practitioners carry but rarely document. For an AI agent, a process atom defines what must happen and what must not, with guardrails built into the definition itself. When a business condition changes, updating the atom propagates that change to every agent using it, centrally, explicitly, and auditably. Decision traces harvested from agents in production can, in turn, be used to refine those atoms over time.

  • Layer 4, Orchestration and Governance Context.

Most AI governance today is post hoc. The consequences of what an agent did are discovered after the fact. This layer inverts the logic. With SAP AI Agent Hub and LeanIX, authorizations, approval structures, and segregation of duties are embedded into the conditions of execution, not implied in an audit trail reviewed after the business has already felt the impact. This is the difference between governance as a document and governance as an architecture layer.

  • Layer 5, Intent and Prescriptive Context: Company Memory.

This is the least discussed but arguably most critical layer in the market conversation about AI governance. Company memory is the governed, persistent repository of organizational behavior knowledge, everything agents need to act correctly, compounding and staying current as the organization evolves. Without it, every new agent and every new workflow must be endlessly re-described. With it, the enterprise gets progressively better at communicating itself to the machines it deploys.

The architecture matters for a reason that goes beyond governance. Every layer built into the context stack is context that no longer needs to be sent at query time. The burden moves out of the agent layer, where it is unstable, expensive, and ungovernable, and into a managed system. Better economics, better governance, and greater scale follow from the same structural decision.

Registration link for access to all the recordings and slide and to join the forthcoming sessions! https://events.sap.com/eaa-autent-web/en_us/home.html

The State of AI: Jason Taylor's Market Context

 

Jason Taylor opened his session with a broader orientation to the AI landscape before drilling into SAP-specific architecture. The growth curve of AI capability has been extraordinary. From AlexNet's early image recognition in 2012, to the "Attention Is All You Need" transformer architecture paper from Google in 2017, to the emergence of the first true large language models and the explosion that followed, the pace of change has been genuinely unprecedented. Models are now multimodal, capable of extended reasoning, and operate with context windows exceeding one million tokens. RAG enables access to data outside model training. MCP enables external tool calling. Agent AI, a model in a loop with tools, memory, and a goal, has arrived as the defining architecture of the current era.

The challenge Jason was clear about: impressive as these capabilities are, the enterprise production bar is substantially higher than a prototype or a single-user deployment. Security, explainability, identity and access management, observability, auditability, the full set of non-functional requirements that good architects have always required, apply with equal force in the agentic era.

The North Star Architecture and Joule Work

Jason grounded the broader AI discussion in SAP's AI-Native North Star Architecture, a four-layer vision that provides the structural context for where Joule Work sits. At the top, an adaptive UX layer with Joule as the central engagement point. Below it, an agentic process layer where every application becomes a capability provider for agents. Below that, a foundation layer serving up data and business context. At the base, a platform layer hosting applications and agents reliably. Agent-to-agent protocol governs how agents communicate. MCP servers handle tool calling. BDC Connect facilitates zero-copy data sharing.

Joule Work represents a fundamental inversion in how enterprise software operates. Rather than navigating between applications to accomplish work, users express intent in plain language, and Joule pulls the data, coordinates the agents, and runs the work across every connected system, SAP and non-SAP. Three experiences define the interface:

  • Conversations, intent-led dialogue, the familiar chat surface
  • Spaces, dynamic, AI-generated work environments that adapt content to user intent and context, surfacing relevant information for real-time analysis without the noise of irrelevant data
  • Develop, the developer environment for building, testing, and deploying AI agents and applications with Joule Studio

For enterprise architects, the implication is substantial. Designing an engagement architecture now requires accounting for AI orchestration as a first-class element. That means headless access to underlying system capabilities and a strong API-first posture. Organizations that invested early in API governance will find themselves ahead of the curve.

Behind the interface, each autonomous domain, Finance, Supply Chain, Procurement, HCM, Customer Experience, brings specialized assistants and agents built on deep process knowledge, data context, and governance frameworks. Jason illustrated this with a lead-to-cash example: agents can handle structured, repeatable tasks across closing activities, replenishment cycles, sourcing events, and service resolutions, while maintaining enterprise controls and full auditability. People focus on strategic thinking and judgment calls. Agents handle the structured work in between.

Joule is also certified to ISO/IEC 42001, the international standard for responsible development, deployment, and operation of AI systems, and works within established SAP role-based access controls. Customer data is not shared with third-party LLM providers for model training.

 

The SAP Knowledge Graph: Making Agents Genuinely Useful

The SAP Knowledge Graph provides the semantic foundation that separates agents that are confidently wrong from agents that reason accurately over how the business actually operates.

It links natural language inputs to SAP's structured metadata, spanning API definitions and endpoints, business semantics (what revenue means, how customer relates to order, how processes connect across domains), data product metadata including schema, lineage, freshness, and authorization rules, and customer-specific extensions including custom fields, custom APIs, and tenant-specific configurations. The graph runs on SAP HANA Cloud and uses open standards: Resource Description Framework (RDF) and Web Ontology Language (OWL). It traverses knowledge across the full SAP portfolio and can be enriched over time as agent decision lineage and human corrections are fed back into it.

The practical effect: agents do not have to infer, guess, or apply generic training data that is not relevant to a specific organization. Finance, sales, procurement, and supply chain are understood as an integrated whole, with relationships, interdependencies, and domain-specific meaning built into the context the agent reasons from. Hallucinations decrease. Decisions can be trusted and acted upon.

For architects and data teams, Jason's guidance was clear: treat the semantic architecture as a first-class design element. Canonical business object definitions, enterprise-wide semantic standards, and quality metadata governance will directly determine how useful the Knowledge Graph becomes as an agent substrate

Three Imperatives for Architects

Both speakers converged on a clear set of responsibilities for the enterprise architecture community:

Own the context stack. The architecture that supports managed, governed context for AI is not an infrastructure decision to be delegated. It is an architectural one that requires EA leadership. Without it, context will continue to be improvised at runtime, unstable, expensive, and ungovernable.

Operationalize AI governance as architecture, not policy. The difference between a governance policy document and a governance architecture layer is the difference between discovering what an agent did and determining what an agent can do before it fires. EAs have the tools, the mandate, and the positioning to lead this shift.

Anchor on outcomes, not outputs. Translate AI activity into AI capability, with measurable business value, clear ownership, and the structural accountability that turns a pilot into a production system. Jason's framing was direct: we all lived through big data, RPA, low-code/no-code, and microservices. Each required EA to be the tip of the spear for thoughtful, value-anchored adoption. Agentic AI is no different.

What Comes Next

Session 4 moves from architecture to build. The focus will be Joule Studio, covering its architecture, the path from intent to deployment-ready agent, and a live hands-on demonstration with the product team.

If you are designing for the autonomous enterprise, this is the session where the blueprint meets the development environment.

Registration link for access to all the recordings and slide and to join the forthcoming sessions! https://events.sap.com/eaa-autent-web/en_us/home.html

Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise is a 13-episode webinar series. Recordings, session summaries, and community discussions are posted to the SAP Community Enterprise Architecture Group after each session.

entry.title: Your AI Agent Has Three Different Definitions of "Customer." That's a Problem.
entry.content:
Source: IDEA Forum, NYC 2025

My last post made one argument: most AI projects don't stall because of the model. They stall because of the data underneath it. This post is about the most common culprit - the one that tends to surprise people, because they thought they'd already solved it.

---

## Three records. One customer. Zero clarity.

Here's the scenario.

You deploy an internal AI copilot connected to your CRM, billing system, and logistics platform. A sales manager asks it to pull together everything the company knows about a key account before a renewal meeting. The agent returns an answer - confident, formatted, comprehensive.

And it's wrong. Not because the AI failed. Because the customer has three different records across three different systems, each updated independently, none of them authoritative.

This isn't a data quality story. It's a structural one. Most large organizations carry fragmented or duplicated records for every core business entity - customers, vendors, products, employees. Humans have always navigated that fragmentation with judgment: they know which system to trust for which question. That institutional knowledge is invisible, undocumented, and entirely absent from the AI.

The agent doesn't apply judgment. It uses whatever it can access, and it uses it with confidence.

---

## What the ODI framework calls this

The ODI's Framework for AI-Ready Enterprise Data treats this as a baseline condition of enterprise data, not an edge case. It names Master Data Management, establishing a single authoritative "golden record" for each core entity, as one of six enterprise-specific criteria that separate organizations ready to scale AI from those that aren't.

The framing matters. MDM isn't new. Most SAP shops have some version of it in place. What the framework adds is a recast: MDM is no longer a data governance discipline. In an agentic world, it's AI infrastructure.

Without it, every AI system querying across your data is operating on an unverified, inconsistent foundation. The errors that produces are systematically difficult to trace. You won't see them at first. You'll see them six months in, when someone starts pulling on a thread and finds the model was confident about the wrong version of reality.

---

## What MDM at scale actually requires

The framework is specific about what this takes operationally. Designating records as authoritative isn't enough. You need entity resolution pipelines — continuous processes that actively scan across disparate systems, identify duplicates, and merge them into validated, governed golden records. Not a one-time clean-up. An ongoing operational capability.

That distinction matters. Organizations that treat MDM as a project to complete are the same ones rebuilding it two years into an AI initiative, under pressure, with a more complicated data landscape than they started with.

---

## Where SAP Business Data Cloud sits in this

This is the design premise of SAP Business Data Cloud: harmonized, business-ready data objects that unify SAP application data, S/4HANA, SuccessFactors, Ariba, and the broader SAP ecosystem, into a governed, consistent layer that AI systems can trust.

The logic is direct. AI performs reliably only on data it can verify. You can't verify data without a single authoritative source. Business Data Cloud is the mechanism for establishing that source - not as a static warehouse, but as a live, governed business data layer that persists as AI workloads run against it.

For anyone evaluating or implementing Business Data Cloud: the ODI framework gives that investment independent validation. Harmonizing business objects, resolving duplicates, establishing governed master records, that's not data hygiene. It's the prerequisite for every AI use case you're planning to build on top of it.

---

## The compounding problem

There's a specific risk the framework names worth sitting with. AI systems don't just consume data, they generate it. Classifications, summaries, derived records, confidence scores. As deployments scale, AI-generated data accumulates alongside source data. Without MDM, that generated data starts to obscure original truth, and the further you get from that moment, the harder it becomes to identify which record is authoritative.

The framework calls this the "single source of truth" problem compounding under AI load. The organizations that address it before scaling will have a significantly easier time than those that retrofit it afterward.

How many authoritative versions of "customer" does your organization actually have right now? Most people I ask pause before answering.

---

## Two ways to go deeper

The ODI framework publishes at the end of June. IDEA membership is free - it includes early access to the full report and a peer community of senior data leaders working through exactly these questions.

[Join IDEA]

On June 25th, the ODI and SAP are hosting a live webinar, Building the Foundation: AI-Ready Data for the Modern Enterprise,  working through the framework directly with the team that built it.

[Register for the June 25th webinar]

---

IDEA (the Interchange for Data and Enterprise AI) is a programme of independent research and peer learning developed in partnership between SAP and the Open Data Institute. Nine roundtables. 140+ senior executives. Three research briefs. And a framework that's about to change how enterprise AI readiness gets measured.

entry.title: The Architecture Question Your AI Strategy Can't Avoid
entry.content:
IDEA Forum - Copenhagen, 2025

The most common architectural response to an AI readiness problem is the wrong one: consolidate everything into a single system, run AI on top of it, declare the foundation solved.

It doesn't work.

Large organizations don't have a consolidation problem. They have a connection problem. A customer exists differently in three systems. A product code means one thing in finance and something adjacent in supply chain. That fragmentation is structural,  built over years of acquisitions and org changes. Centralizing it would take years and introduce a single point of failure.

The right answer isn't to move the data. It's to connect it.

---

## What the framework points to

The ODI's Framework for AI-Ready Enterprise Data,  publishing this month through the IDEA programme, defines 21 concrete criteria across four pillars. Read across them, and a pattern emerges. The requirements don't point toward centralization. They point toward connection: connecting distributed data without moving it, enriching it with semantic context, governing it in a way that holds for autonomous agents as well as humans.

That pattern has a name: a data fabric.

A data fabric is not a product, there's no software you install and declare yourself AI-ready. It's an architectural approach: integration pipelines, active catalogues, and governance engines working together to make distributed data usable. It leaves the data where it is and creates a unified virtual access layer on top - harmonizing contents at the point of query, surfacing a verified, governed response to whoever or whatever is asking.

The ODI's supplementary research on data fabrics, released alongside the main framework, maps this to three specific capabilities: integration, cataloguing, and governance.

---

## Why it matters for SAP organizations specifically

Each of those three capabilities has a direct read-across to the SAP data landscape.

Integration is where entity resolution lives, continuous matching across connected systems, merging fragmented records into the golden records AI can actually trust. SAP Business Data Cloud operationalizes this for the SAP ecosystem, harmonizing data objects across S/4HANA, SuccessFactors, Ariba, and the broader application landscape into governed, consistent business entities.

Cataloguing is where most organizations are further behind than they think. A static data catalog is a directory, useful for humans, insufficient for AI. What the framework calls for is active cataloguing: machine learning continuously profiling data as it arrives, tracking lineage, building a semantic map that AI agents can traverse to understand not just what data exists, but how everything relates.

Governance is the capability that scales worst without intentional architecture. Traditional governance was built for humans who read policies and exercise judgment. AI agents don't do that. The framework's answer is policy-as-code: access rules enforced at the infrastructure level, before the model ever sees the data.

---

## The timing

The framework publishes this month. On June 25th, the ODI team (Elena Simperl, Paul Groth, and Stuart Coleman) will present it live and take questions. One hour, free.

[Register for the June 25th webinar]

And if you want early access to the full report before it's publicly indexed:

[Join IDEA]

---

The organizations that figure out the connection problem are the ones that will scale AI reliably. Everyone else will keep running pilots.

Where does your organization sit? Have you started the architectural conversation, or is it still downstream of the model selection debate?

---

IDEA — the Interchange for Data and Enterprise AI — is a programme of independent research and peer learning developed in partnership between SAP and the Open Data Institute. Nine roundtables. 140+ senior executives. Three research briefs. And a framework that changes how enterprise AI readiness gets measured.

entry.title: Improved Data Action scoping with the new Data Filter Parameter type
entry.content:
Overview

Designing powerful planning and analytics processes requires more than static member selections. In many real-world scenarios, planning users rely on dynamic story filters and input controls, including those with exclusions, time ranges, and attribute-based filtering to scope of their data updates and reporting activities.  Up until now, however, these advanced filter selection scenarios could be defined in the story but could not be fully leveraged when executing data actions. Rather, consumption of story filters and input controls in data actions was restricted to those filters using explicit member lists (or the All member). 

To cover dynamic selection scenarios designers often had to use workarounds which necessitated significant maintenance or rely on the users to select appropriate members based on a static list.  In many cases, this would lead to inconsistencies between what users saw or selected in a story and the scope of execution processed by the data action, with the net effect of processing of more data than should be required.  Enter the data filter parameter.

 

Introducing the Data Filter Parameter

With the introduction of data filter parameters (releasing with our Q3/2026 QRC), SAP Analytics Cloud takes a major step forward in making data actions’ consumption of parameter values more flexible, consistent, and aligned with the way users work in stories.  Data filter parameters help to reconcile what the user selects and visualizes (including dynamic filter selections) with what the data action processes.   It represents a new parameter type that allows any number of dimension filters to be passed as-is into a single parameter, while allowing the filter selection to cover to dynamic story filters and input controls, including support for:

  • Exclusions (“everything except…”)
  • Dynamic date ranges or custom current date selections
  • Attribute based dimension filters

Instead of resolving an individual static member-list for each dimension that needs to be filtered and passing those to separate parameters, data actions (and multi action executing data action steps) can now capture the filter definition from the story or input control while preserving its full semantics, and do so across multiple dimensions within a single parameter.

 

Configuring Data Filter Parameters

Data filter parameters are configured like other parameters within the data action, but they differ from other dimension-based parameters in that the parameter itself is dynamic.  Specifically, the design-time does not involve specifying which dimensions, elements, or filters will be passed to the parameter (there is an exception to this for Advanced Formulas, which we will discuss). Rather, that is determined in story design time based on the specific input controls and/or story filters assigned to the parameter within a given data action (or multi action) starter.   In this way, the parameter itself is much more flexible than traditional dimension-based parameters, allowing it scale to filter a single dimension selection or any number of dimensions (including support for multiple filters on the same dimension), while also allowing these filters to differ completely based on the specific calling trigger.

Figure 1

Figure 2

As with other dimension-based parameters, data filter parameters are applied in the context area of data action step types, including:

  • Copy
  • Cross-model copy
  • Currency conversion
  • Deletion
  • Structured allocation

In these scenarios, the filter acts as a precise scoping mechanism, determining which data is read and processed while retaining the full target logic and calculation capabilities of the step.

Figure 3

In Advanced Formula steps, two new configuration options are available:

  1. CONFIG.DATA_FILTER_PARAMETERS – used to activate one or more filter parameters to be used in the Advanced Formula step
  2. CONFIG.DATA_FILTER_DIMENSIONS - to define exactly which dimensions from the filter apply should apply and can be used in place of MEMBERSET to cover dimension filtering 

Note that while the other steps will automatically filter based on all dimensions fed into the parameter in the story trigger, Advanced Formulas require explicit definition of some, or all, of the included dimensions via the CONFIG.DATA_FILTER_DIMENSIONS setting.   Reference to explicit data filter-parameter dimensions/elements can also occur via DATA(), DELETE(), and RESULTLOOKUP statements within the Advanced Formula. However, where this occurs, the explicit configuration option CONFIG.HIERARCHY also needs to be set for those dimensions. 

Figure 4

Summary

The data filter parameter reflects SAP’s ongoing investment in aligning analysis and planning actions within a single, consistent user experience. By allowing data actions (and multi actions that call them) to consume an expanded array of dynamic story filters and do so within a single parameter whose scope can vary based on the specific calling instance, data filter parameters help simplify data action design, use, and performance.  As planning scenarios continue to grow in complexity, this enhancement ensures that data actions scale with the sophistication of SAP Analytics Cloud stories—without sacrificing clarity or control.

entry.title: Turning Spreadsheet Logic into a Scalable Calculation Framework in SAP Analytics Cloud
entry.content:
Challenge:

We were working with a grid-like structure seen below, where 

  • Each row = Post ~ Shed Location (0–21)
  • Each column = Bay (0–28)
  • Data was available only at selected locations (shaded cells)
  • The remaining cells needed to be calculated

     

Across 4 compartments (51–54), this created hundreds of calculation points.

The real challenge was simple to ask, but hard to implement:

How do we calculate values for all empty cells using nearby available cells 
without writing and maintaining hundreds of formulas?

How it looked in Excel?

Let’s take one example from the sheet  : Cell M86Excel formula:  

=((J86/POWER(24,2)) + (N86/POWER(8,2)) + (M83/POWER(15,2)) + (M89/POWER(15,2))) / ((1/POWER(24,2)) + (1/POWER(8,2)) + (1/POWER(15,2)) + (1/POWER(15,2)))

What does this really mean? 

To calculate one cell, we take values from nearby cells, but closer ones influence the result more.

In this grid:

  • Each Bay = 8 metres
  • Each Post = 5 metres

So for M86:

Source

Distance

Impact

 

J86

24

Small

N86

8

Large

M83

15

Medium

M89

15

Medium

Then we:

  • Multiply each value by its weight
  • Add everything
  • Divide by total weight

This is essentially a distance-based weighted average

The problem with Excel:

While this works…

  • Every cell has a different formula
  • It quickly becomes hard to maintain
  • Not scalable across compartments
  • Impossible to reuse inside SAC directly

What we did in SAP Analytics Cloud?

Instead of copying formulas everywhere, we converted the logic into a data-driven model

 

Step 1: Store the logic as data (Config Table)

Instead of writing formulas, we created a config table.

Each row represents one target cell.

For example (for M86):

 For each cell, we store , for our example of cell M86 :

Target → Compartment = 52, Bay 4, Post 18

power 1 = 1/(24^2) = 0.0017361,

Source1 =Compartment = 52 Bay 1, Post 8

Power 2 = 1/(8^2) = 0.0156250

Source2 =Compartment = 52, Bay 5, Post 18

Power 3 =  1/(15^2) = 0.0044444

Source 3 :- Compartment =52,Bay =  4, Post =  21

Step 2:- Use ONE SAC Calculation

Now SAC runs a single reusable logic for all cells:

Result = (Value1 × Power1 + Value2 × Power2 + Value3 × Power3 + Value4 × Power4) / DenomAdd

  • No hardcoding 
  • No duplication 
  • Works for every compartment

Step 3 - Control the Calculation Flow

We added a simple control flag “StickyCardNotPlaced” in the Config dimension:

  • 3 → Base rows (input-driven rows)
  • 4 → Key columns (input-driven Cols)
  • 5 → Remaining cells

 This ensures:

  • Correct calculation order
  • No circular dependencies
  • Stable results

SAC Story (End Result)

Why this approach works

Instead of managing hundreds of formulas:

  •  We moved logic into data (config)
  •  Built one reusable calculation
  •  Made the model flexible and scalable
  •  Simplified maintenance and validation

Rather than replicating Excel formulas, we transformed the logic into a config-driven framework and let SAC handle the calculations dynamically.

This approach allowed us to turn a complex spreadsheet model into a scalable SAC solution.

Happy to connect if you’re working on similar grid-based or planning scenarios

entry.title: Context Aware Data Action Scoping
entry.content:
Overview

Data actions are a critical component of end-to-end planning workflows in SAP Analytics Cloud, supporting everything from seeding, to allocations, to complex calculations. Despite their importance, one lingering question has persisted: how can we optimize the data action to process only the minimum data required based on user actions?

Until now, users have not had a straightforward mechanism for discrete change tracking at the planning record level - no built-in way to identify which specific dimension members or data was actually changed in a version during a planning session. Progress has been made in related areas: the Planning Area concept significantly improved the ability to limit the scope of data brought into a Public Edit session (and thus processed by a data action), and scripted approaches using the onAfterDataEntryProcess table event have allowed developers to capture changed members and pass them to data actions via parameters (see The onAfterDataEntryProcess event in SAC).  While certainly helpful, these either do not address this core issue of data action processing, or do so in a way that requires custom development effort, carries some risk, and are not always transparent.

Without a native way to know exactly what changed, the result has been that data actions are often scoped more broadly than necessary, which can lead to longer run times and consumption of unnecessary resources. That changes with our Q3/2026 QRC release.

 

Introducing Change-Context Data Action Scoping

Change-context data action scoping builds on the new Data Filter parameter capability, also introduced in our Q3/2026 QRC release (see Improved Data Action scoping with the new Data Filter Parameter type for a full overview).  It allows the data action author to use a data filter to define specific dimension(s) along which to track user updates (such as Cost Center, Product, or Time). During runtime, the system automatically identifies which members within the designated filter dimensions have changed data in the target version (or another designated version), and those members then become the filter scope.  No custom scripting, no workarounds - just a simple configuration option which tells SAC to automatically scope data action processing to the dimension elements where data that was actually changed by the user.

 

Using Change-Context Scoping

Change tracking is easily enabled within the data action during definition of the data filter parameter. When adding a new parameter, the data action author selects two inputs within the configuration: the dimensions to track changed members on (for example, Cost Center, Time, or Product), and the version to inspect for changes (either a fixed version such as Budget 2026, or a reference to a version parameter such as the target version). These are configured within the Input section of the parameter definition by changing the Visibility setting to Private, after which the data action author can add the specific dimension filter(s) and the relevant version for change tracking. At runtime, SAP Analytics Cloud identifies which members of the dimension(s) have modified records in the reference version and uses those as the filter scope.

If multiple dimensions are selected within the filters, then the data action will run on the cartesian product of impacted members across the applicable dimensions.

Note: The change-context filter should be configured as a dedicated data filter, distinct from any filters and input controls used to filter the story data. Setting the data filter parameter type to Private, and then selecting the ”Auto-Determined by version changes” ensures it operates as the change-tracking filter. Once configured, as with other parameters, the change-context data filter needs to be applied to the filter section for the relevant data action steps where filtering is required.

The change context members accumulate across runs — subsequent executions will include members changed in earlier runs within the same version, and resets on version publish.

 

Figure 1: Parameter Definition

Figure 2: Parameter Assignment

The included screenshots illustrate a scenario with a data action that performs a currency conversion and has configured change-context tracking enabled on the EB_OPEX_CC (cost center) dimension.  When the user enters data changes on the Information Technology cost center (see below), the data action runtime parses the user updates and detects Information Technology as the only cost center dimension element that has modified records, and scopes the execution accordingly (as can be seen in the Job Monitor for the specific execution).

Figure 3: Data Action Execution

 

Figure 4: Job Monitor

Summary

Change-context data action scoping is a meaningful step forward in making planning workflows in SAP Analytics Cloud more intelligent and efficient. By equipping data actions with native awareness of user changes, calculations automatically target only what’s been modified—streamlining processing and boosting responsiveness.   Easily configured in the data action designer with just a dimension selection and reference version, it eliminates the need for custom scripting or other complex workarounds.

 
We’d love to hear how it works for you—please share your experiences and feedback in the comments below.

entry.title: From Prototype to Production with Joule Studio: Recap of Session 4 of Architecting the AE Webinar
entry.content:
The Enterprise Readiness Gap

If you are using Claude Code, Cursor, or any modern agentic development environment, you can build a working agent in hours. It will look impressive. It will handle the use case you designed it for. And it will have almost nothing in common with what an enterprise deployment actually requires.

Getting an AI agent to production in an enterprise context means: aligning business stakeholders, IT, and architecture before a single line of code is written. It means authentication, role-based access control, and single sign-on handled correctly. It means enterprise-grade infrastructure that scales, testing that covers edge cases and evaluates actual agent behavior, audit trails that satisfy compliance requirements, and monitoring that continues long after go-live.

The gap between a prototype and an enterprise-ready agent is not a matter of effort. It is a structural difference in what the two things are built for.

That gap is what I called 'The Enterprise Readiness Gap', and it is precisely what Joule Studio is designed to close.

SAP's positioning is straightforward: Joule Studio is the complete environment to build, run, deploy, manage, and govern agentic AI solutions at enterprise scale.

 

The right to win rests on three capabilities that are difficult to replicate outside of SAP's ecosystem. First, an intent-based development environment that supports everything from natural language vibe coding to full pro-code with your own IDEs and frameworks. Second, business context built directly into both design and runtime: SAP Knowledge Graph, Signavio, LeanIX, Cloud ALM, domain models, and backend systems connected through MCP servers. Third, a trusted governance layer where observability, logging, audit, and lifecycle management are built into the platform, not bolted on after the fact.

Where Joule Studio Sits

Joule Studio is the development pillar inside Joule Work, SAP's new engagement layer. Joule Work provides three surfaces: Conversations, Spaces, and Development. Joule Studio is the Development surface, accessible via browser, desktop, or mobile. The architecture brings together a design-time environment, an SAP-managed runtime, and governance connections to LeanIX, Cloud ALM, and Signavio for agent evaluations, tracing, and performance monitoring.

Three builders are available in the design-time environment today: an Agent Builder for Python-based conversational agents, a Workflow Builder powered by an SAP-n8n partnership, and a CAP App Builder for UI5-based applications. Each produces standard, portable, Git-versionable output, not proprietary artifacts.

Joule Studio Architecture

 For teams who want to work in familiar tooling, VS Code, Claude Code, and Cursor can all connect into the same Joule Studio project. There is no migration, no copy-paste handover. You work in the web editor when you want speed, switch to your preferred IDE when you want depth, and everything stays in sync.

You can get access to all the session recordings and slides by registering here: https://events.sap.com/eaa-autent-web/en_us/home.html

The Six-Phase Lifecycle: From Intent to Production

Julia Kloppenburg walked us through the complete intent-driven development lifecycle, end to end, and then demonstrated it live on a working agent. The six phases represent a structured approach to close enterprise readiness gap.

Phase 1: Intent. Everything starts with a business outcome described in natural language. What needs to change? Who is affected? What does success look like? Joule Studio captures this as a durable problem statement, enriches it with real enterprise context, from Signavio process maps to LeanIX architecture data to SAP Knowledge Graph semantics, and carries it forward through the entire lifecycle. The intent does not get lost in the handover between business and IT. It becomes the thread that connects every subsequent phase.

Phase 2: Product Requirements Document. From the grounded intent, Joule Studio generates a full PRD: problem statement, target users, success measures, requirements grouped by priority, user stories, acceptance criteria, solution architecture, and milestones. What makes this different from a standard requirements exercise is that the requirements reflect how the business actually runs, grounded in real process and architecture data rather than theoretical assumptions. This phase also forces an early decision: activate standard SAP capabilities, configure what already exists, or build something new.

Phase 3: Specification. The PRD becomes an implementation-ready blueprint. Scope, architecture, integrations, security, task breakdown, business object definitions, and system contracts are all defined here. Critically, compliance and policy requirements are embedded at this stage. Safety is built in, not applied after the fact. This is the point where technical choices and integration patterns are locked down, which eliminates the rework loops that typically consume later phases of development.

Phase 4: Code Generation. The build agent generates agents, workflows, and CAP applications directly from the specification, with SAP context integrated throughout: the right domain models, the right integration approach, N8N where orchestration is part of the solution. The full code base is exposed, rewritable, and Git-versionable. Developers own the output. Teams can inspect it, refactor it, and apply their normal engineering discipline to it.

Phase 5: Testing. Joule Studio validates the end-to-end flow, not isolated components. Automated evaluations assess whether the agent behaved as intended, not just whether it ran. A single test report provides coverage, behavior checks, and per-component failure signals. This is the checkpoint that makes the deployment decision a confident one rather than an optimistic one.

Phase 6: Deployment and Operations. The full solution is pushed to GitHub and deployed to SAP-managed runtime. SAP handles provisioning, scaling, monitoring, and day-to-day operations. Cost and usage signals are tracked continuously in production. The AI Agent Hub serves as the central governance control plane: discover agents, manage their lifecycle, observe behavior, trace decisions, and respond to issues. Deployment is not the end of the story. It is where the improvement loop begins.

Julia demonstrated the full lifecycle on a real system, not a recording, using a new hire onboarding agent built entirely in Joule Studio.

The use case is familiar to anyone who has joined a new organisation: navigating tasks, finding documents, completing mandatory training, and getting answers to questions without continuously interrupting HR teams. The agent handles all of that conversationally, surfaces relevant documents on demand, tracks onboarding task completion, and escalates unresolved queries to HR via notification.

The demo started with the intent phase, where Joule Studio generated an intent fit score of 88 percent from a name and a short description. It showed the auto-generated problem statement, goals, and metrics, with the Signavio recruit-to-retire process visible as business context. The PRD contained detailed requirements, user stories, and solution architecture. The specification included MCP integration details and agent behavior definitions. The test suite covered structural, server, and agent tests, all of which passed. The deployment view showed the agent live, connected to SAP SuccessFactors via Agent-to-Agent protocol, running on SAP BTP.

The point of the demo was not the use case itself. It was the coherence of the process: a single environment that carries the intent from natural language through to a deployed, tested, governed production agent, with the business context embedded throughout.

 Key Clarifications

Joule Studio and SAP Build are not the same thing, and Build is not being replaced. SAP Build continues unchanged: customer-managed, supported. Joule Studio is SAP-managed, AI-first, and designed for intent-driven agentic development. 

What you can build: Conversational agents, system-triggered agents, deterministic CAP applications, N8N workflows, and multi-agent systems. The platform is not limited to agentic use cases.

What Comes Next

Session 5 on July 1 turns to the question of how SAP's Innovation Factory approaches architecting and activating AI at the enterprise level with Helmut Grimm, Raghu Banda, Jose Augusto Tornaro, and Vishal Mehta to unpack the methodology behind moving from architecture planning to active AI deployment.

If Session 4 answered how Joule Studio works, Session 5 addresses how the broader activation journey is structured across an enterprise engagement.

You can get access to all the session recordings and slides by registering here: https://events.sap.com/eaa-autent-web/en_us/home.html

entry.title: Recreating Excel‑Like Heat Maps in SAP Analytics Cloud Using Dynamic User‑Driven Thresholds
entry.content:
If you’ve worked with Excel heat maps, you already know how effortless it feels.

Just select your data → apply Conditional Formatting → Color Scales, and instantly you get a beautiful gradient showing highs, lows, and patterns. The colors automatically react to the values simple, intuitive, and powerful.

Excel :- 

But when you try to replicate the same behavior in SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC), things start to get interesting.

The Challenge in SAC

 In SAC, you can create heat maps and apply conditional formatting using thresholds. However:

Heat map charts rely on a single measure for color intensity
Conditional formatting in tables works with:
      -  Number ranges (static) OR
     -   Comparison to another measure (dynamic)
The real limitation comes when:  You want the gradient to adapt dynamically based on user-defined thresholds, like min/mid/max inputs similar to Excel flexibility.

Out-of-the-box, SAC doesn’t directly provide this level of interactive gradient control in a native heat map.

 So the question becomes:

Can we mimic Excel’s behavior using SAC building blocks?

Yes, and that’s exactly what this solution does.

 Solution Overview

 Instead of relying on the native SAC heat map, the approach shifts to:

          Using a Table + Calculated Measure + Conditional Formatting

This gives us full control over how colors are applied

 Step 1: Design the Data Structure

In the main SAC table:

Measure X → Holds the actual values (the data you want to visualize)
Values are stored at specific intersections (e.g., based on dimensions like Bay, Post, etc.)
Additionally, create 3 input measures:

  • Minimum
  • Midpoint
  • Maximum

These are not part of the main data grid, but stored at separate intersections where users can input values

Step 2: Enable User Input for Thresholds

Users enter:

  •  Minimum threshold
  •  Midpoint threshold
  •  Maximum threshold

This allows complete flexibility; users can dynamically control how the gradient behaves based on their scenario.

Step 3: Create the Logic – “XColorScore”

1) Created a Restricted Measure "RM-X" restricted  to its intersection at a a story level 

2) Created 3 Restricetd Measures one for each Measure restricted to its intersection at a story level

  •  Minimum (RM - Min)
  •  Maximum (RM - Max)
  •  Midpoint (RM - Mid)

3) Create a calculated measure (XColorScore) that:

Compares each Measure X value against Minimum, Midpoint, and Maximum

Example logic (conceptually):

If X <= Minimum → assign lower score
If X ≈ Midpoint → assign medium score
If X >= Maximum → assign higher score
Essentially, you convert your raw values into a color scoring scale.

This follows the SAC best practice of deriving a numeric output to drive conditional formatting

 

Step 4: Apply Conditional Formatting

Now comes the clever part.

In SAC:

  • Select Measure X 
  • Go to Conditional Formatting (Thresholds)
          Choose:  Compare To → Measure 
                            Comparison Measure → XColorScore

Since thresholds in SAC can compare one measure against another, this enables dynamic, data-driven coloring logic instead of static ranges.

 

Step 5: Achieve the Gradient Effect

By defining appropriate ranges in the threshold:

Green → lower values
Yellow → mid-range
Red → higher values
You effectively simulate:

  •  A heat map
  • A gradient scale
  • Based on user inputs


Why This Approach Works

This design leverages three powerful SAC concepts:

Separation of Data & Logic
→ Raw data (X) vs Colour logic (XColorScore)
User-driven input
→ Thresholds are not hardcoded
Measure-to-measure comparison
→ Unlocks dynamic conditional formatting

 

 Key Takeaways

Native SAC heat maps are great, but not always flexible enough
Conditional formatting with measure comparison is the real game changer
By introducing a scoring layer, you can simulate Excel-like gradients
Most importantly:  You give control back to the business user

Closing Thoughts

Moving from Excel to SAC often exposes these small gaps in flexibility.

But with a bit of creative modelling, SAC can go beyond Excel,especially when you combine:

  • Planning input
  •  Calculated measures
  •  Conditional formatting

This solution is a perfect example of that.

entry.title: Provisioning a Solution in SAP Data Accelerator: A Step-by-Step Guide
entry.content:
Hi, community!

SAP is introducing SAP Data Accelerator, an SAP-managed service that enables secure access to data from supported SAP systems through authorized partner solutions. It replaces the customer-managed connectivity setup used in supported partner access scenarios with an SAP-operated service layer designed to support enterprise security, regulatory compliance, and operational availability, while helping reduce brittle integrations, simplify operations, and minimize transition effort.

This guide walks you through the end-to-end process of provisioning a new solution in SAP Data Accelerator using SAP for Me. It is intended for administrators and technical users who need to set up and access a new SAP Data Accelerator tenant. By the end of this guide, you will have a fully provisioned solution and the credentials to sign in to it.

Step 1: Open SAP for Me

Open SAP for Me in your browser and navigate to Portfolio & Products > Applications and choose Start Provisioning in the SAP Data Accelerator Hub tile. 

SAP for Me dashboard showing Applications in Products & Portfolio

Step 2: Use the Create Solution Wizard

The Create Solution wizard opens and displays four steps: General Information, Select Applications, Configure Parameters, and Review. The wizard starts at Step 1: General Information. Wait for any loading indicator to finish before proceeding.

Step 3: Enter General Information

Fill in the required information: Name (1), Path (2), and choose Next Step (3).

General Information page

Step 4: Enter Configure Parameters

Fill in the required information: Region, Cloud Connector Auth Data Password, External System Name, Certificate (PEM): copy and paste the entire certificate from "begin" to "end", External System Name, Certificate (PEM): copy and paste the entire certificate from "begin" to "end", and choose Review.

Step 5: Review the Information

Review the information entered and if everything looks correct, choose Finish.

Step 6: Download the Authentication Data

You will receive a pop-up screen informing you that provisioning has started and that the resources are being provisioned in the selected resource group. Choose the View in Resources button to check the status of your request. While the tenant is in Processing state (1), a View Notifications button (2) becomes available. Use this button to access the Download connection details link (3).

Tenant Notifications screen

Step 7: Connect the Cloud Connector to SAP Data Accelerator 

The authentication data downloaded from your SAP Data Accelerator instance includes the subaccount details required for the setup in Palantir's platform, such as the region, subaccount, login email address, and password.

Subaccount details

Use the information from those details to connect the Cloud Connector to the SAP Data Accelerator subaccount, as described in the SAP Help Portal page Connecting to SAP Data Accelerator. Complete this step within 5 minutes of downloading the authentication data. The activation link expires 1 hour after it is generated.

Step 8: Start Using SAP Data Accelerator 

Once the account has been activated, your SAP Data Accelerator is ready to use (1). You can log in to your SAP Data Accelerator either using your SAP Data Accelerator URL or using the Main URL (2) provided in the Access Information of the SAP Data Accelerator tenant.

Account ready to use

The purpose of this blog post is to guide you step by step through the process of provisioning a solution in SAP Data Accelerator. For a list of the prerequisites and more information, I suggest you visit the SAP Help Portal documentation Provisioning SAP Data Accelerator.

For more information about SAP Data Accelerator, see SAP Data Accelerator product page.

If you liked reading this post, feel free to share it with your colleagues. Also, I invite you to follow my profile and leave a comment or a question. 

All the best,

Eduardo

entry.title: Slides & Recordings from the 13-Part 'Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise' Webinar Series
entry.content:
Dear All, 

So far we have got tremendous support and response to the 'Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise' Webinar Series.  We have got multiple requests for having slides and recordings from the already completed sessions of the 13-Part 'Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise' Webinar Series. 

 
The Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise webinar series is a 13-part community learning journey running weekly from June through August 2026. Each session brings SAP architects, product leaders, and customers together to unpack the What, Why, and How of the Autonomous Enterprise, from the Business AI Platform and agent development to data foundations, governance, and the Context System.
 
It is architects learning together, with content evolving based on community feedback. Sessions cover the practical architectural decisions you need to make today to prepare for an agentic world.
 
Join us live or catch up on-demand.  All sessions are complimentary and open for all. You can join us in the future sessions by registering here: https://events.sap.com/eaa-autent-web/en_us/home.html
 
We are publishing the recordings and slides from every session because this series is a shared learning journey, and learning only works if the materials are accessible to everyone, whether you attended live, joined late, or are catching up between sessions. Below are the recordings and slide decks from the Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise webinar series, updated after each session.
 
Do consider sharing with your colleagues , customers and partners and join us for the future sessions!

Regards,
Gaurish Dessai, Ralph Richter, Jason Luo & Raghu Banda
Global Enterprise Excellence Team at SAP.

entry.title: From Silos to Strategy: What 20 Years of SAP Data Management Taught One Practitioner
entry.content:
Insights from a conversation with Rakesh Ramakrishnan, Principal Development Consultant at SAP and veteran SAP data management practitioner and consultant

When it comes to data management, there’s no shortage of frameworks, tools, and buzzwords competing for attention. But talk to someone who has spent two decades in the trenches of SAP implementations, and the noise falls away quickly. What's left is a set of hard-won principles every data practitioner should internalize before their next project kicks off.

I recently sat down with Rakesh Ramakrishnan, a longtime SAP practitioner who started his career as a C and Visual C++ programmer before an accidental encounter with an SAP customer in Malaysia in the year 2000 that changed his trajectory. Twenty years later, working as a development lead in the data management space in North America, Rakesh has a clear point of view on what separates successful data management implementations from the ones that stumble.

Start with the Customer's Vision—Not the Data

Ask Rakesh what a successful data management implementation looks like, and he doesn't start with tools or even data. He starts with the customer.

"It all starts with the customer's vision and long-term goal. To fulfill that strategy, what capabilities are required? Within those capabilities, what end-to-end processes are running? And when you come to the level of process, that is where you discover what sort of data is flowing between them."

Data is a mandatory ingredient of any successful implementation—but it's not the starting point. Skip business strategy, and you'll spend the rest of the project reworking things you shouldn't have built.

The Silo Mindset Is Still the #1 Killer of Data Programs

Rakesh's most direct warning was about how organizations think about data:

"Most people think about data in a siloed environment. If procurement is there, the procurement team is only looking at what data they want. They're not looking at where the data is coming from, who's consuming it, or whether something is missing that could impact subsequent processes."

The result? Failure that cascades through the entire customer vision. In SAP's world, he emphasized, "we never talk about silos—we always talk end-to-end process." Data governance, lineage, and design decisions must reflect that end-to-end reality.

Governance Must-Haves: Data as a Whole, Lineage, and Footprint

When asked which governance capabilities are non-negotiable, Rakesh was refreshingly clear:

"To me, it is data as a whole—master data and transactional data—along with data lineage and data footprint. That is what is more critical."

If you understand what data each process requires, where it originates, and which processes should receive it, you can pass only the necessary data to only the necessary processes. Overloading downstream processes with unnecessary data is a recipe for confusion and risk. Before diving into cataloging, classification, or business data fabric conversations, make sure your data lineage and footprint story is airtight.

The Real Lesson from a Successful Project: Step Out of the Comfort Zone

Rakesh's proudest project was a retail SAP S/4HANA implementation at a wholesale trade company, spanning sales, procurement, and finance. What made it stand out wasn't the technology, it was the method.

During the Explore phase, workshops ran from 8 a.m. through the afternoon. Rather than passively "taking data in," Rakesh introduced a collaborative flip-chart mechanic: each business domain maintained its own flip chart, and at the end of every workshop day, each domain presented processes, requirements, and gaps. The result was a unified picture of processes and the data flowing between them, giving the architect visibility into cross-topic dependencies.

His larger point:

"As a team member, it becomes our responsibility to step out of the comfort zone and think from the process perspective. Every rework has an impact on timelines. If you spend some time upfront, you reduce rework and unnecessary risks."

Data professionals who stay strictly in the "load and reload" lane will always be part of the problem. The ones who understand process become indispensable.

Get the Right People in the Room — Especially the End Users

Who should be part of the data conversation? Rakesh was emphatic: the end users (that’s you - the data professionals!).

"On the source side from the customer end, it is mostly the end users. The presence of the department head is also required, but it is primarily the end user, because he or she is the one playing around with this data."

The project mentioned earlier succeeded because business users and department heads were pulled in alongside subject matter experts to answer critical questions: Is this the right data? Is this information really mandatory?

Where the project had loopholes — unsurprisingly — was in data profiling and harmonization. Skipping these topics guarantees pain during execution.

Choose the Right Tool for the Right Job

One of the most candid parts of the conversation was about tool selection. Rakesh's favorite feature on the SAP S/4HANA side? The SAP Migration Cockpit.

"Migration Cockpit comes with standard templates in line with the back-end tables or structures. SAP gives you the right template so the data goes right into that particular thing."

But Migration Cockpit isn't the answer to everything. SAP Signavio provides the complete process picture.  And SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) is powerful and ever-evolving solution for AI and next-generation data scenarios.

The Foundation Comes Before the Autonomy

As the industry naturally is buzzing about the autonomous enterprise, Rakesh's closing thought was grounded:

"All concepts are good. We have all these beautiful tools. But the only thing required is properly structuring it out."

You can't have an autonomous enterprise without a data foundation. And you can't build that foundation without doing the unglamorous work: aligning to business strategy, breaking silos, enforcing lineage, involving end users, profiling and harmonizing data, and picking the right tool for each job.

Final Thoughts

If there’s one thread running through Rakesh’s twenty years of experience, it’s this: great data management is less about technology and more about awareness, awareness of business goals, of end-to-end processes, of who consumes what data, and which tools truly fit the problem.

For those of us implementing data management solutions, it is about having the discipline to slow down at the start, ask the right questions, and bring in the right people in order to build a data foundation that will actually support what comes next — whether that’s an SAP S/4HANA migration or the autonomous enterprise of tomorrow.

What’s the #1 lesson from your data & analytics/AI implementations? Drop it in the comments.

entry.title: SAP Business Data Cloud and Datasphere News in June
entry.content:
SAP Business Data Cloud and Datasphere News in June

June was a full month. Three releases shipped back to back, the team delivered across a wide range of areas, and the German SAP user group DSAG kept the conversations grounded with honest feedback and real customer examples at their AK Business Analytics meeting in St. Leon-Rot.

Learn more in my top features video in June 2026 on YouTube 📺.

In addition, explore the latest updates and blogs in my community news blog.

 

My top features in June

Use Object Store Sources in Replication Flows

You can now use object store sources directly in replication flows. Amazon S3, Azure Data Lake Gen2, Google Cloud Storage, SFTP, HANA Data Lake Files, and Microsoft OneLake are all supported. The data lands in local table files in Datasphere. If your raw data lives in cloud storage, you no longer need a separate ingestion pipeline.

➡️  More information

 

 

Use Oracle as a Source in Replication Flows

Oracle is now supported as a source in replication flows, with Initial Only load type. Full snapshot replication from Oracle to your supported targets in Datasphere. Oracle Wallet authentication is also now supported, enabling SSL and certificate-based authentication via mTLS.

➡️  More information

 

 

Use Snowflake as a Source in Replication Flows

You can now create Snowflake connections and use them as a source in replication flows, also with Initial Only load type. For non-public Snowflake environments, Cloud Connector is supported as a reverse invoke proxy, routing traffic through a TLS tunnel.

➡️  More information

 

 

Cloud Connector Support for Confluent Cloud Connections

You can now configure Cloud Connector for Confluent Cloud connections. Same principle as Snowflake: Cloud Connector routes traffic through a TLS tunnel so replication flows can reach Confluent Cloud without public endpoints. Requires Cloud Connector to be set up in your landscape.

➡️  More information

 

 

SQL View Transform in Transformation Flows on File

You can now create a SQL view transform inside a transformation flow on file, using Apache Spark SQL. A full SQL editor with auto-complete, drag-and-drop source objects, real-time validation, and data preview. All of this running directly in your file space. This capability was explicitly listed as not supported until this release.

➡️  More information

 

 

Direct Delta Sharing Client Integration for Transformation Flows

Accessing delta share tables in transformation flows, from BDC Foundation Services or Databricks, no longer requires an intermediate step. The integration is now direct, with no intermediate copying, better performance, and reduced storage overhead.

 

 

CLI Extensions: Pause/Resume Replication Flows + File Space Import/Export

Two CLI improvements in one release. You can now pause and resume replication flows and individual replication objects via the command line. File space objects are also now accessible from the CLI: import and export of spaces, modeling objects, tasks, and connectivity. Fewer things that require manual UI interaction for teams running Datasphere through automation.

➡️  More information

 

 

Enhanced Capacity Unit Monitoring

The Capacity Unit Monitoring tool now has a customizable date and time range filter. No longer limited to the current month. A new Spaces tab shows consumption broken down per space, covering Premium Outbound Integration, object store usage, and total capacity unit consumption. Useful for anyone managing multi-space environments.

➡️  More information

 

 

Enable System Notifications ⭐ KP's Pick

You can now configure email notifications for replication flow and task chain completions directly in your user profile settings, for success, failure, or both. No more manual monitor checking to find out if an overnight run completed. Small feature, real impact.

➡️  More information

 

 

YOUTUBE LINK

 
 

DSAG AK Business Analytics — June Meeting in St. Leon-Rot

On June 19 I joined the DSAG AK Business Analytics working group in St. Leon-Rot for their summer session. These meetings have a specific quality that's hard to find elsewhere. The audience is technical, the questions are direct, and nobody is there to be sold to.

The agenda this time was particularly strong:

▶️  SAP Business Data Cloud roadmap including fresh SAPPHIRE announcements (Fabian Hartje)
▶️  SAP Analytics Cloud roadmap update (Matthias Kraemer)
▶️  AI in practice: how Swisscom uses AI in the SAP ecosystem (Tjarko von Lehsten, Viktor Seide)
▶️  BDC Object Store implementation with real-world examples (Stefan Härtelt)
▶️  360° BDC — Data Provisioning, SAC and Planning, Parts I and II (Maria Alejandra Uruena, Sandy Tran, Jie Deng)

Fresh SAPPHIRE updates, live demos, and real customer examples in one room. That's a good day. Thanks to AK speakers Jörg Steinhorst and Stefan Kahle for organizing, and to everyone who showed up and contributed to the discussion.

 

My favorite blogs in June

SAP Analytics Cloud is expanding live data connectivity beyond the classic live models. @david_stocker explains how BigQuery, Snowflake, and Azure SQL are already supported, with SharePoint and more cloud data lakes coming later this year. No replication, no import jobs. Just a live connection via Smart Data Access and virtual tables behind the scenes. The blog also explains how this underpins Seamless Planning. A good orientation read if you haven't looked at live data connectivity yet.
🔗 Live Data Connectivity in SAP Analytics Cloud - Overview and Direction

@AndreasForsterpublished a hands-on tutorial for vibe coding SAP HANA Cloud Machine Learning with SAP Business Application Studio, Python, and an AI coding agent. Instead of writing the PAL/APL code yourself, you describe what you want and let the agent generate it. Jupyter notebooks and all. The example is a time-series forecast, but the setup works for any ML scenario HANA Cloud supports. He also shows how to add current documentation context to keep the generated code accurate.
🔗 Hands-on Tutorial: Vibe coding SAP HANA Cloud Machine Learning with BAS, Python, Claude and Cline

CDC-based replication flows in Datasphere don't use ODP queues at all. @anilkumarauce traces the full internal architecture step by step: changes are captured at database table level using logging tables, triggers, and a staging layer called the Resilient Data Buffer, all the way from an S/4HANA application table into Datasphere. Exactly the kind of under-the-hood knowledge that saves hours when something unexpected happens in production.
🔗 Under the Hood of ABAP CDC in SAP Datasphere Replication Flows

The most complete provisioning reference for SAP Business Data Cloud on SAP Community. @ShreyasriB built out an 8-part step-by-step series covering the full provisioning journey: from the initial BDC Cockpit setup via SAP for Me through Datasphere, SAC, Databricks, HANA Cloud, Intelligent Apps, and Snowflake. The kind of guide you want open in a second browser tab while doing the actual setup.
🔗 Provisioning of Business Data Cloud: Start with Part 1

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Custom ABAP CDS View to Datasphere: Replication Flow Setup, Status & Delta Processing
entry.content:
Introduction: 

This technical guide explains how to replicate data from a custom ABAP CDS view in SAP S/4HANA into SAP Datasphere using Replication Flows with Change Data Capture (CDC). It covers the concepts, the end-to-end setup, how deltas are captured, the related jobs and system tables, and what to expect during initial and delta runs.
You’ll learn:
  • How RMS, APE, and CDC work together
  • How to prepare a CDC-enabled custom CDS view
  • How to configure a Replication Flow in Datasphere for Initial only and Initial + Delta loads
  • What artifacts (logging and buffer tables, jobs) are created in S/4HANA and how to monitor them
  • How to validate inserts, updates, and deletes end-to-end
  • SAP Datasphere local table, Internal delta capture columns (Change Date or Change Type).

Basics:

  • Replication Management Service (RMS): Orchestrates change data capture lifecycles, creates and monitors master/subscriber logging tables, and runs real-time jobs for observing and pushing deltas. Manages the end-to-end reliability of triggers and logging.
  • ABAP Pipeline Engine (APE): An ABAP-based runtime used by Datasphere to extract from ABAP sources (S/4HANA tables or CDC-enabled CDS views). Acts as the “consumer” in CDC terms for Replication Flows. Supporting Initial Only, Delta Only & Initial and Delta options.
  • Change Data Capture (CDC): Uses database triggers to record INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE changes on base tables that back an ABAP CDS view.Captured changes flow from: Base table triggers → Master Logging Table → Subscriber Logging Table → Buffer Table → Replication Flow into Datasphere target.

Prerequisites and Preparation:

Authorizations:
  • ABAP development access to create a CDS view
  • Access to SM37, DHADM, DHCDCMON, DHRDBMON for monitoring
  • Permissions in SAP Datasphere to create and run Replication Flows
System readiness:
  • RFC/ODP or ABAP connection from Datasphere to S/4HANA (as per your Datasphere connection setup)
  • Sufficient background work processes for real-time jobs
SAP S/4HANA RMS job settings, scheudling and operational tuning:

Standard jobs (auto-created/managed, visible in SM37; commonly run under DDIC)

  • /1DH/CDC_HEALTH_CHECK: Periodic CDC health verification.
  • SAP_DHCDC_DISPATCHER: Dispatcher for CDC orchestration.
  • /1DH/OBSERVE_LOGTAB: Moves changes from Master Logging Table to Subscriber Logging Table. Default reschedule interval ~1 hour.
  • /1DH/PUSH_CDS_DELTA (aka /1DH/CDS_PUSH_DELTA): Joins subscriber logs with base table and writes Buffer Table. Default reschedule interval ~6 hours.

Tuning guidance:

  • Start with defaults, then shorten intervals if your delta SLAs require it and your system resources allow.
  • Coordinate with Basis to adjust job class/priority and periodicity to avoid long-run job lists and ensure timely delta movement.
  • Monitor DHCDCMON (log table depth) and DHRDBMON (buffer latency). If backlogs build, increase job frequency and/or background work processes.

Connectivity:

  • ABAP or SAP S/4HANA Connection between SAP Datasphere and SAP S/4HANA.
  • SAP Datasphere + SAP S/4HANA: Your Guide to Seamless Data Integration.
  • SAP Cloud Connector for SAP S/4HANA (Private/ On-prem).
Design recommendations: Prefer SAP standard released CDS views or SAP data products where possible for lifecycle stability. If not then create custom ABAP CDS view/entity, data products instead of using direct ABAP tables as context information is lost and significant effort is required to reverse-engineer the logic. 
  • SAP Datasphere: Extract, Transform & Load (ETL) - Best Practices

1. Create a CDC‑Enabled Custom CDS View

The example below shows a simplified custom ABAP CDS view Z_SALESORDER on top of a custom base table ZVBAK. It enables analytical usage and data extraction, and configures CDC mapping so deltas are captured.

Sample reference code:

@AbapCatalog.sqlViewName: 'ZISDSALESORDER'
@AbapCatalog.compiler.compareFilter: true
@AbapCatalog.preserveKey: true
@EndUserText.label: 'Z Sales Order'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@ObjectModel: {
   compositionRoot:   true,
   representativeKey: 'SalesOrder',
   usageType: {
     dataClass:      #TRANSACTIONAL,
     serviceQuality: #B,
     sizeCategory:   #L
   },
   supportedCapabilities: [ #ANALYTICAL_DIMENSION, #CDS_MODELING_ASSOCIATION_TARGET, #SQL_DATA_SOURCE, #CDS_MODELING_DATA_SOURCE, #EXTRACTION_DATA_SOURCE ],
   modelingPattern: [ #ANALYTICAL_DIMENSION ]
}
@ObjectModel.sapObjectNodeType.name: 'SalesOrder'

@Analytics: {
    dataExtraction: {
        enabled: true,
        delta.changeDataCapture: {
            mapping:[
                {
                    table: 'zvbak', role: #MAIN,
                    viewElement: ['SalesOrder'],
                    tableElement: ['vbeln']  }
            ]
        }
    }
 }
define view Z_SALESORDER as select from zvbak
//Association
  association [0..*]...{<Columns>};

For testing, I developed a custom ABAP program to perform CREATE, UPDATE, and DELETE operations on the ZVBAK custom table and validate end‑to‑end propagation. I initially inserted 10 records.

Use transaction SE16N on the CDS SQL view ZISDSALESORDER to view the record count.

2. Create a Replication Flow in SAP Datasphere.

Replication Flows are the recommended mechanism for high‑volume replication into SAP Datasphere and supported targets.

2.1 Note: If you choose Initial Only mode, the target will have the same columns as the source (130 columns in this case).

2.2 When using the Initial and Delta mode, SAP Datasphere adds two internal columns (Change_Type and Change_Date) to the Z_SALESORDER local table. The following Change_Type values are typically observed in ABAP CDS replication. Replication Flow Scenarios with Different Load Types.

OperationChange_Type
Initial Load L
Pure Insert I
Delta Load (UPSERT: insert if not exists, else update by key )A 
Delta Load (DELETE)D 

Note: The delta capture table is an internal SAP Datasphere object whose structure may change at any time and is not released for external access.It is only used by SAP Datasphere internal applications (for example, Transformation Flows, Replication Flows etc.)Using the internal delta capture columns (Change_Date or Change_Type) or their content for any external delta replication outside Premium Outbound Integration is not permitted. 

3725855 - Delta Columns CHANGE_TYPE and CHANGE_DATE not visible in Data Viewer or Data Products for Local Tables in HDLF Spaces in SAP Datasphere

3634750 - Unexpected behavior with delta enabled Datasphere local table and models using the same feature

2.3  The Replication Flow’s delta frequency is configured for every 5 minutes (default: 1 hour). You can modify this as required. Using 0 hours and 0 minutes switches to near real‑time.

 

3. Execute the Replication Flow for the first time to perform the Initial Load

3.1 In DHADM, you can see that a new subscriber and buffer table are created in SAP S/4HANA for CDS Z_SALESORDER (check via T‑Codes DHADM and DHRDBMON). This occurs when you run the Replication Flow for the first time. Subscriber ID: C30227C32B841FE19EF81806E972D0BE; Producer Type: CDC; Consumer Type: APE (ABAP Pipeline Engine).

3.2 Master Loggining Table /1DH/ML000000024 and Subscriber Logging table /1DH/SL000000024 got created and can also be monitored in the Transaction DHCDCMON.

Master Logging Table: /1DH/ML<XXXXXXXXX>, It captures delta records from triggers as the single source of truth.
